serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of flavoured black tea is approximately 250 ml (one standard bottle or cup), providing around 97.5 kcal and 24.25 g of carbohydrates including 22 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured black tea (no sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D09020022 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for flavoured black tea with no added sugar containing 8.8 g sugar/100 g. GI estimated at 55 by analogy with sugar-containing fruit-flavoured tea beverages and sucrose-based beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (<=55) per the University of Sydney classification scale (low: <=55, medium: 56-69, high: >=70). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 24.25) / 100 = 13.3. Analogous estimate documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage effect: 8.8 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB beverage threshold; sodium is low at 4 mg/100 g but sugar independently raises blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Despite the 'no sugar' label, this beverage contains 8.8 g sugar per 100 ml — classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage — which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; individuals with hypertension should choose plain unsweetened black tea or water instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (8.8 g/100 g) in liquid form with rapid glycaemic absorption; GI 55 with GL ~13 per 250 ml serving constitutes a significant glucose load for a beverage.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This flavoured black tea contains 8.8 g sugar per 100 ml — equivalent to approximately 22 g sugar per standard 250 ml serving — which constitutes a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA guidelines and is not recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; plain unsweetened black tea (0 g sugar) is the preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification (8.8 g sugar/100 g) with negligible saturated fat and very low sodium; the primary CVD concern is the sugar load rather than fat or sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages including this flavoured black tea (8.8 g sugar/100 ml) is associated with increased cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance pathways; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake and prefer plain unsweetened black tea which retains cardioprotective polyphenols without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory sugar load (8.8 g/100 g) negates the anti-inflammatory polyphenol benefit of black tea; no qualifying anti-inflammatory nutrient factor present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The 8.8 g sugar per 100 ml in this flavoured black tea promotes inflammatory mediators that negate the theaflavin and thearubigin polyphenol benefit of black tea; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose plain unsweetened black tea to obtain anti-inflammatory polyphenols without the pro-inflammatory sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Black Tea (No Sugar) is a commercially prepared ready-to-drink beverage consisting of brewed black tea infused with natural or artificial fruit flavourings and no added refined sugar. Despite the 'no sugar' label, this product contains 8.8 g of sugar per 100 ml — likely derived from natural fruit sugars present in the flavouring concentrates — which classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA clinical definitions. Per 100 ml, it provides 39 kcal, 0 g protein, 0 g fat, 9.7 g carbohydrate (of which 8.8 g is sugar), 1.41 g dietary fibre, and 4 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg. The beverage is low in sodium and calories relative to many soft drinks but is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar content. Black tea naturally contains theaflavins and thearubigins — polyphenolic antioxidants with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties — but these benefits are substantially offset by the sugar load in this flavoured variant. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification) by analogy with sugar-containing fruit-flavoured tea beverages, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.3 per 250 ml serving. This product is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or all-day beverage in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Consumers seeking the health benefits of black tea polyphenols should choose plain unsweetened black tea with 0 g sugar.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D09020022, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this canonical approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate documented per policy.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero — this is a tea-based beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is confirmed zero — this beverage contains no fat-bearing ingredients by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same source. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific flavoured black tea product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D09020022. The 'no sugar' label on this product refers to no added refined sugar; however, natural fruit sugars from flavouring concentrates contribute 8.8 g sugar per 100 ml, classifying this as a sugar-sweetened beverage under clinical guidelines. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-containing fruit-flavoured tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney scale (low <=55, medium 56-69, high >=70); no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. GL per 250 g serving = (55 × 24.25) / 100 = 13.3.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language, addressing G6 and Q1 failures. Added these new local-language names to the flat aliases array to ensure consistency with aliases_by_language, addressing Q8 failure. Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 ontology honesty requirements.
Iteration 2: Confirmed gi_category 'medium' was set for GI 55 — validator flagged this as incorrect.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category per University of Sydney classification scale (low: <=55, medium: 56-69, high: >=70). Updated glycemic_load_per_serving to 13.3 using full carbohydrate content (55 × 24.25 / 100). Updated gi_source notes and notes field to document the GI classification rationale. Updated natural_language_description to reflect corrected gi_category.
